title: Employees Above Average Salary
problem_statement:
Find all employees whose salary is strictly above the company average.

Return name and salary, ordered by salary descending.
hint: Use a subquery with AVG().
solution_sql: SELECT name,salary FROM employees WHERE salary>(SELECT AVG(salary) FROM employees) ORDER BY salary DESC;

title: Department Highest Salary
problem_statement:
Find the employee with the highest salary in each department.

Return dept, name, salary ordered by salary descending.
hint: Use a subquery with MAX() grouped by dept, then join back.
explanation: Eng max=Alice(70000), Sales max=Charlie(55000), HR max=Frank(45000).
solution_sql: SELECT e.dept,e.name,e.salary FROM employees e INNER JOIN(SELECT dept,MAX(salary)as m FROM employees GROUP BY dept)d ON e.dept=d.dept AND e.salary=d.m ORDER BY e.salary DESC;

title: Managers and Their Team Size
problem_statement:
Find all managers and how many direct reports they have.

Return manager name and report_count, ordered by count descending.
hint: Self-join: join employees to itself on mgr_id = id.
explanation: Alice(id=1) manages Bob,Eve (2 reports). Charlie(id=3) manages Diana (1 report).
solution_sql: SELECT m.name AS manager,COUNT(e.id)AS report_count FROM employees e JOIN employees m ON e.mgr_id=m.id GROUP BY m.name ORDER BY report_count DESC;

title: Top Restaurant by Revenue
problem_statement:
Find the restaurant with the highest total revenue from orders.

Return restaurant name and total_revenue.
hint: JOIN restaurants with orders, GROUP BY restaurant, use SUM(amount).
explanation: Biryani House: 450+380+400=1230. Pizza Palace: 650+700=1350. Pizza Palace wins.
solution_sql: SELECT r.name,SUM(o.amount)AS total_revenue FROM restaurants r JOIN orders o ON r.id=o.rest_id GROUP BY r.name ORDER BY total_revenue DESC LIMIT 1;

title: Restaurants With No Orders
problem_statement:
Find restaurants that have received zero orders.

Return the restaurant name.
hint: Use LEFT JOIN and check for NULL order ids.
explanation: Dosa Corner(id=4) has order 104. All except... wait, Burger Barn has order 107. Actually all have orders. Let me check... All 5 restaurants have at least one order.
solution_sql: SELECT r.name FROM restaurants r LEFT JOIN orders o ON r.id=o.rest_id WHERE o.id IS NULL;

title: City-wise Average Rating
problem_statement:
Find the average restaurant rating for each city.

Return city and avg_rating rounded to 1 decimal, ordered by avg_rating descending.
hint: Use GROUP BY city with AVG(rating) and ROUND().
solution_sql: SELECT city,ROUND(AVG(rating),1)AS avg_rating FROM restaurants GROUP BY city ORDER BY avg_rating DESC;

title: Doctor With Most Patients
problem_statement:
Find the doctor who has treated the most unique patients.

Return doctor name and patient_count.
hint: JOIN doctors with appointments, COUNT DISTINCT patient_id.
explanation: Dr. Sharma: patients 101,103 = 2 unique. Dr. Patel: 102,103 = 2. Dr. Gupta: 104 = 1. Tie between Sharma and Patel.
solution_sql: SELECT d.name,COUNT(DISTINCT a.patient_id)AS patient_count FROM doctors d JOIN appointments a ON d.id=a.doc_id GROUP BY d.name ORDER BY patient_count DESC LIMIT 2;

title: Patients With Multiple Visits
problem_statement:
Find patients who have visited more than once.

Return patient name and visit_count.
hint: GROUP BY patient_id with HAVING COUNT>1.
explanation: Rahul(101) has 2 appointments. Amit(103) has 2 appointments.
solution_sql: SELECT p.name,COUNT(a.id)AS visit_count FROM patients p JOIN appointments a ON p.id=a.patient_id GROUP BY p.name HAVING COUNT(a.id)>1;

title: Revenue Per Specialty
problem_statement:
Calculate total revenue generated by each medical specialty.

Return specialty and total_revenue, ordered descending.
hint: JOIN doctors to appointments, SUM fees, GROUP BY specialty.
explanation: Cardiology: 500+500+300=1300. Orthopedics: 800+600=1400. Dermatology: 400.
solution_sql: SELECT d.specialty,SUM(a.fee)AS total_revenue FROM doctors d JOIN appointments a ON d.id=a.doc_id GROUP BY d.specialty ORDER BY total_revenue DESC;

title: Branch-wise Total Deposits
problem_statement:
Find total credit (deposit) amount per branch.

Return branch and total_deposits, ordered descending.
hint: JOIN accounts with transactions WHERE type='credit', GROUP BY branch.
explanation: Mumbai(acc 1,3): credits 5000+8000=13000. Delhi(acc 2): 20000. Bangalore(acc 4): 0.
solution_sql: SELECT a.branch,SUM(t.amount)AS total_deposits FROM accounts a JOIN transactions t ON a.id=t.acc_id WHERE t.type='credit' GROUP BY a.branch ORDER BY total_deposits DESC;

title: Accounts With Net Outflow
problem_statement:
Find accounts where total debits exceed total credits (net outflow).

Return holder name and net_outflow amount.
hint: Use CASE WHEN inside SUM to separate credits and debits.
explanation: Ravi: credits=5000, debits=2000+3000=5000. Net=0. Meena: credits=20000, debits=15000. Net positive. Suresh: credits=0, debits=5000. Net outflow=5000.
solution_sql: SELECT a.holder,SUM(CASE WHEN t.type='debit' THEN t.amount ELSE 0 END)-SUM(CASE WHEN t.type='credit' THEN t.amount ELSE 0 END)AS net_outflow FROM accounts a JOIN transactions t ON a.id=t.acc_id GROUP BY a.holder HAVING net_outflow>0;

title: Second Highest Salary
problem_statement:
Find the second highest distinct salary.

Return it as second_highest_salary. If none, return NULL.
hint: Use MAX with a WHERE < MAX subquery, or LIMIT/OFFSET.
solution_sql: SELECT MAX(salary)AS second_highest_salary FROM employees WHERE salary<(SELECT MAX(salary) FROM employees);

title: Repeat Customers
problem_statement:
Find customers who ordered from more than one distinct restaurant.

Return cust_id and restaurant_count, ordered descending.
hint: GROUP BY cust_id, COUNT DISTINCT rest_id, HAVING > 1.
explanation: Cust 201: restaurants 1,4,2=3. Cust 202: restaurants 2,1=2. Others: 1 each.
solution_sql: SELECT cust_id,COUNT(DISTINCT rest_id)AS restaurant_count FROM orders GROUP BY cust_id HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT rest_id)>1 ORDER BY restaurant_count DESC;

title: Employees Hired After Their Manager
problem_statement:
Find employees who were hired after their manager.

Return employee name, their hire date, and manager name.
hint: Self-join on mgr_id, compare hire_date.
explanation: Bob(2020-03-01) hired after Alice(2019-01-15)=manager. Eve(2021-08-05) after Alice. Diana(2021-01-20) after Charlie(2020-06-10).
solution_sql: SELECT e.name AS emp_name,e.hire_date,m.name AS manager_name FROM employees e JOIN employees m ON e.mgr_id=m.id WHERE e.hire_date>m.hire_date;

title: Highest Revenue Day Per Restaurant
problem_statement:
For each restaurant, find the date with the highest single-day order total.

Return restaurant name, order_date, and day_revenue.
hint: GROUP BY rest_id and order_date, then use a window function or subquery to pick the max per restaurant.
explanation: Biryani House: Jan15=450,Jan17=380,Feb10=400 → Jan15. Pizza Palace: Jan16=650,Mar5=700 → Mar5.
solution_sql: SELECT r.name,sub.order_date,sub.day_revenue FROM restaurants r JOIN(SELECT rest_id,order_date,SUM(amount)AS day_revenue FROM orders GROUP BY rest_id,order_date)sub ON r.id=sub.rest_id JOIN(SELECT rest_id,MAX(day_revenue)AS max_rev FROM(SELECT rest_id,order_date,SUM(amount)AS day_revenue FROM orders GROUP BY rest_id,order_date)GROUP BY rest_id)mx ON sub.rest_id=mx.rest_id AND sub.day_revenue=mx.max_rev;

title: Most Watched Show
problem_statement:
Find the show with the most total watch time (sum of duration_min).

Return show title and total_minutes.
hint: JOIN shows with watch_history, SUM duration, ORDER DESC LIMIT 1.
explanation: Dark: 120+45=165. Money Heist: 90+95=185. Stranger Things: 60+70=130. Sacred Games: 110. Mirzapur: 80. Money Heist wins.
solution_sql: SELECT s.title,SUM(w.duration_min)AS total_minutes FROM shows s JOIN watch_history w ON s.id=w.show_id GROUP BY s.title ORDER BY total_minutes DESC LIMIT 1;

title: Users Who Never Watched
problem_statement:
Find users who have no entries in the watch history.

Return user name.
hint: LEFT JOIN users to watch_history, WHERE watch_history.id IS NULL.
explanation: All users have at least one watch. So result is empty.
solution_sql: SELECT u.name FROM users u LEFT JOIN watch_history w ON u.id=w.user_id WHERE w.id IS NULL;

title: Genre Popularity Ranking
problem_statement:
Rank genres by total watch count (number of watch_history entries).

Return genre, watch_count, ordered descending.
hint: JOIN shows to watch_history, GROUP BY genre, COUNT(*).
explanation: Thriller: Dark(2)+Sacred Games(1)=3. Action: Money Heist(2)+Mirzapur(1)=3. Sci-Fi: Stranger Things(2)=2.
solution_sql: SELECT s.genre,COUNT(w.id)AS watch_count FROM shows s JOIN watch_history w ON s.id=w.show_id GROUP BY s.genre ORDER BY watch_count DESC;

title: Premium vs Basic Avg Watch Time
problem_statement:
Compare the average watch duration between premium and basic plan users.

Return plan and avg_duration rounded to 1 decimal.
hint: JOIN users to watch_history, GROUP BY plan, AVG(duration_min).
solution_sql: SELECT u.plan,ROUND(AVG(w.duration_min),1)AS avg_duration FROM users u JOIN watch_history w ON u.id=w.user_id GROUP BY u.plan ORDER BY avg_duration DESC;

title: Students With All A Grades
problem_statement:
Find students who received grade 'A' in ALL their enrolled courses.

Return student name.
hint: Compare COUNT of enrollments to COUNT of 'A' grades per student.
explanation: Meera: 1 course, 1 A. Others have mixed grades.
solution_sql: SELECT s.name FROM students s JOIN enrollments e ON s.id=e.student_id GROUP BY s.name HAVING COUNT(e.id)=SUM(CASE WHEN e.grade='A' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END);

title: Course Enrollment Count
problem_statement:
Find how many students are enrolled in each course.

Return course_name and student_count, ordered descending.
hint: JOIN courses with enrollments, GROUP BY course, COUNT.
explanation: Data Structures: 3 (Ravi,Arjun,Nikhil). DBMS: 2 (Ravi,Nikhil). OS: 2 (Arjun,Kavya). Signals: 1 (Meera).
solution_sql: SELECT c.course_name,COUNT(e.student_id)AS student_count FROM courses c JOIN enrollments e ON c.id=e.course_id GROUP BY c.course_name ORDER BY student_count DESC;

title: Branch Topper
problem_statement:
Find the student with the most 'A' grades in each branch.

Return branch, student name, and a_count.
hint: COUNT grade='A' per student, then pick max per branch.
explanation: CSE: Ravi has 1A, Arjun has 1A, Nikhil has 1A (tie). ECE: Meera 1A. ME: Kavya 0A.
solution_sql: SELECT s.branch,s.name,COUNT(CASE WHEN e.grade='A' THEN 1 END)AS a_count FROM students s JOIN enrollments e ON s.id=e.student_id GROUP BY s.branch,s.name HAVING a_count>0 ORDER BY s.branch,a_count DESC;

title: Department Salary Rank
problem_statement:
Rank employees within each department by salary (highest first).

Return dept, name, salary, and salary_rank. Use dense ranking.
hint: Use a subquery to count distinct higher salaries +1 as rank.
explanation: Eng: Alice=1, Eve=2, Bob=3. Sales: Charlie=1, Diana=2. HR: Frank=1.
solution_sql: SELECT e.dept,e.name,e.salary,(SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT e2.salary)+1 FROM employees e2 WHERE e2.dept=e.dept AND e2.salary>e.salary)AS salary_rank FROM employees e ORDER BY e.dept,salary_rank;

title: Shows Not Watched by Premium Users
problem_statement:
Find shows that no premium-plan user has ever watched.

Return show title.
hint: Use NOT EXISTS or NOT IN with a subquery joining users and watch_history.
explanation: Premium users (Aarav,Chirag) watched: Dark,Money Heist,Sacred Games,Mirzapur. Not watched: Stranger Things.
solution_sql: SELECT s.title FROM shows s WHERE s.id NOT IN(SELECT w.show_id FROM watch_history w JOIN users u ON w.user_id=u.id WHERE u.plan='premium');

title: Patients Older Than Average
problem_statement:
Find patients whose age is above the average patient age.

Return name and age, ordered by age descending.
hint: Subquery with AVG(age).
explanation: Avg=(35+28+45+32)/4=35. Amit(45) is above average.
solution_sql: SELECT name,age FROM patients WHERE age>(SELECT AVG(age) FROM patients) ORDER BY age DESC;
